description: Field-level evidence on the research / graduate-school path. Coverage is field-level; admissions and fit outcomes vary widely by sub-field and individual.

items.title: Research aptitude is best tested before committing
items.content: Enjoying coursework is a weak predictor of enjoying open-ended research, which involves long feedback loops, ambiguity, and writing. A short, real research experience (lab, reading group, replication) is a cheaper fit test than committing to multi-year programs. Field-level guidance.

items.title: Advisor and letters are high-leverage admission signals
items.content: For research-track graduate admissions, demonstrated research output and strong letters from researchers tend to carry more weight than grades alone. Building a relationship and a concrete contribution early is high-leverage. Field-level pattern.

items.title: Research paths trade near-term income for deferred optionality
items.content: Funded research programs typically provide a stipend rather than market salary, trading near-term earnings for credentials and deferred optionality (research, specialized industry, academia). The opportunity cost is concentrated in early-career compounding years. Field-level framing.

items.title: Multi-year research commitments are less reversible mid-stream
items.content: Compared with a time-boxed experiment, multi-year research commitments are harder to reverse mid-stream without sunk-cost friction. The reversible window is largely before committing: trial research experiences are cheap; year-three exits are costly. Field-level.
items.citation: Reversibility framing applied to multi-year programs

items.title: Research carries an autonomy-vs-ambiguity emotional profile
items.content: Research offers high autonomy but a distinctive emotional load: long stretches without external validation, self-directed motivation, and tolerance for being stuck. People who are energized by open problems fit better than those who prefer fast, legible feedback. Field-level.
